FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, N=10,054, ages 12-13, Year 3 (2019-2021), US-based

FT_IC2.evidence: Only R-rated movies and mature-rated video games examined. No separately-measured smartphone or social media exposure with own coefficient. TV and gaming alone fail B5 (TV-only, console/unspecified gaming without SM per separate coefficient)

FT_IC3.evidence: Paper does not meet FT-IC2; IC2 failure precludes IC3 consideration

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC2 (not a qualifying exposure). EC7 evaluation not reached.
notes: Paper examines media parenting practices in relation to R-rated movies and mature video games only. No smartphone time, social media use, or other qualifying SM/phone variables measured as outcomes or exposures. Does not qualify for review.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, N=9,519, ages 9-10 at baseline to 12-13 at Year 3 (2016-2021), US-based, longitudinal

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time with separate breakdowns: TV shows/movies, video games (single/multi-player), digital socializing (texting, video chat, social media). Per B1, YSTS papers reporting separate coefficients for SM, video games qualify regardless of TV also included. Qualifies per B1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models for each isometric log-ratio coordinate with time as independent variable. Compositional data analysis with geometric means and regression coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Change-from-baseline estimates with 95% confidence intervals reported throughout (e.g., screen time increased 0.23 h/day from baseline to Year 1, 95% CI: 0.22, 0.24; additional detailed estimates in Table S2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from ABCD Study examining longitudinal changes in screen time, sleep, and sports/exercise activity over four annual assessments

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint, abstract, or duplicate publication

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (IV) in analyses, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 requires smartphone/SM to be the DEPENDENT variable in EVERY analysis. Fails condition (a).
notes: Longitudinal study with four assessments (baseline 2016-2018 to Year 3 2019-2021). Separately reports screen time subtypes (TV, video games, digital socializing) with coefficients. Meets all IC criteria but fails EC7.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, N=2,749, Mage=11.9 at Year 2, Years 2-4 follow-up data analyzed, US-based longitudinal cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Gaming duration (hours per day) and problematic gaming (VGAQ scale) at Year 2 measured. Per B1, gaming duration qualifies as smartphone/app-related variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mediation analysis using R package for causal mediation analysis. Indirect effects, direct effects, and total effects reported with 95% confidence intervals and p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Path coefficients (β), indirect effects, direct effects with 95% CIs reported in Figure 1 and text. Example: caffeine use partially mediated positive relationship between gaming hours and sleep difficulties (indirect effect = 0.023, 95% CI [0.004, 0.049]; direct effect = 0.036)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from ABCD Study examining mediation effect of caffeine use on relationship between gaming duration/problems and sleep

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint, abstract, or duplicate publication

FT_EC7_reason: Gaming duration is the independent variable (IV), not the dependent variable. Additionally, caffeine use is included as a mediator (psychological/behavioral variable), which violates the demographic-only requirement in condition (b). Fails conditions (a) and (b).
notes: Longitudinal study examining mediation effect of caffeine use on gaming-sleep relationship. Gaming is treated as exposure (IV); sleep difficulties and sleep duration are outcomes (DV). Includes non-demographic mediator (caffeine use). Meets all IC criteria but fails EC7.
